findings.description: Top-of-page layout spacing is not preserved on the first page.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, the memorandum header and body begin much higher on the page beneath the letterhead. In the HTML, there is a very large blank area before the title and main content, shifting the visible structure downward.

findings.description: First-page memo formatting is simplified and partially altered.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows more formal memo alignment with labeled fields and cleaner indentation; the HTML keeps the content but changes spacing, label alignment, and heading presentation, making the memo block less visually structured.

findings.description: Section-start layout differs due to excessive whitespace before the content.
findings.evidence: On page 2, the PDF begins directly with section I and II content near the top, while the HTML introduces a large blank area before the section headings, altering page structure though not content.

findings.description: Heading styling and list presentation differ from the source.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses bold serif section headings and compact bullet formatting; the HTML uses sans-serif headings with different weight/size and looser bullet spacing. Content remains intact, but visual distinction is reduced.

findings.description: Content block positioning is shifted downward on the third page.
findings.evidence: The PDF page 3 starts immediately with bullet items and section III, while the HTML again inserts a large blank region before the same content appears, affecting the preserved page layout.

findings.description: Typography differs noticeably in section III and bullet lists.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses bold serif labels within bullets and tighter spacing; the HTML renders these in a different font and spacing, preserving meaning but not the original visual emphasis as closely.

findings.description: Final page layout is shifted with substantial extra whitespace.
findings.evidence: On page 4, the PDF places the continuation bullets and attachments section higher on the page. In the HTML, a large blank area appears before this content, changing the visual grouping.

findings.description: Attachments list formatting is altered from the source.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows the attachments as a roman-numeral list with tabbed alignment in a serif font; the HTML preserves the items but uses simpler sans-serif formatting and different indentation, reducing fidelity to the original presentation.

findings.description: The HTML duplicates the main container element, resulting in nested <main> tags that repeat document wrapper structure unnecessarily.
findings.evidence: HTML begins with '<main>' followed immediately by another '<main>' before Page 1 content. The PDF has no equivalent duplicated structural wrapper.

findings.description: Heading hierarchy is imperfect because top-level section headings are encoded as h2 directly under the document h1, while the memo metadata block ('To', 'From', 'Subject') and page-level structure are not represented semantically. This is not a content loss issue, but the hierarchy only partially reflects the PDF structure.
findings.evidence:
HTML uses '

MEMORANDUM

' and then '

I. History/Background

', '

II. March 10, 2016 Public Meeting and Oral Comments Summary

', etc. The PDF visually shows 'MEMORANDUM' as title and Roman numeral sections beneath it; this is mostly correct, but document structure around the memo header is flattened into paragraphs.
